1: The brief narrative marker between the animal gallery and God's direct challenge to Job — wayyaʿan YHWH ʾet-ʾiyyôḇ wayyōʾmer: the LORD speaks again, directly addressing Job.
2: Hăroḇ ʿim-šadday yissōr: 'Will a critic argue with the Almighty?' — rîḇ (to contend, take legal action): the lawsuit-term. Môkîaḥ ʾĕlôah yaʿănenāh: 'let the one who accuses God answer' — môkîaḥ (the one who reproves, the accuser-plaintiff): Job took on the role of reprover; now he must answer. The challenge is not hostile but genuine: God takes Job's lawsuit seriously enough to invite a response.
3: The narrative marker for Job's first response: wayyaʿan ʾiyyôḇ ʾet-YHWH wayyōʾmer. Job's response is remarkably brief — 2 verses of capitulation — compared to the lengthy speeches that preceded it.
4: Qallōtî māh ʾăšîḇekā: 'I am of little account; what shall I say to you?' — qālal (to be small, of little account): the self-assessment that reverses Job's earlier confident demand. Yādî śamtî lĕpî: 'I put my hand over my mouth' — the silence-gesture of one who has heard enough to know he cannot argue further. The very gesture Job refused to make before the divine voice silences him.
5: ʾAḥat dibbartî wĕlōʾ ʾešneh: 'I spoke once but will not answer again' — the numbered renunciation: once + twice. Ûštayim wĕlōʾ ʾôsîp: 'twice, but I will add nothing more' — the finality. Job does not recant his claims of innocence; he simply falls silent before the divine voice. This is response without refutation.
6: The second divine speech opens with the same theophanic formula as 38:1: YHWH ʾet-ʾiyyôḇ miṣ-śĕʿārāh — from the storm. The continued storm-setting grounds both speeches in the same sustained divine encounter.
7: ʾĔzār-nāʾ kĕgeber ḥălāṣêkā: 'Brace yourself like a man/warrior' — the same challenge as 38:3. ʾEšʾālĕkā wĕhôḏîʿēnî: 'I will question you, and you will answer me' — God is not done; the second speech intensifies the challenge by moving from creation to justice.
8: ʾAp tāper mišpāṭî: 'Would you discredit/nullify my justice?' — pārar (to break, nullify, frustrate): would Job break apart the divine justice-system? Tĕrišʿēnî lĕmaʿan tiṣdāq: 'would you condemn me so that you can be righteous?' — the zero-sum framing: for Job to be righteous, would God have to be guilty? The question cuts to the heart of Job's dilemma — and the answer the whole book gives is: No. Both can be vindicated.
9: Wĕʾim-zĕrôaʿ kĕʾēl lāk: 'Do you have an arm like God?' — zĕrôaʿ (arm, strength): the arm as symbol of royal power. ûḇĕqôl kāmōhû tarʿēm: 'can you thunder with a voice like his?' — the theophanic voice of thunder as divine speech-power (cf. Ps 29). These are not taunts but genuine tests of what would be required for Job to administer justice himself.
10: ʿĂḏeh-nāʾ gāʾôn wāgōḇah: 'Adorn yourself with majesty and height' — gāʾôn (majesty, pride): the same term used for divine majesty (Ps 93:1) and dangerous human pride. Wĕhôḏ wĕhāḏār tilbaš: 'dress yourself in glory and splendor' — hôḏ wĕhāḏār: the divine-attribute pair of honor + majesty (Ps 96:6). God invites Job to try on the divine attributes.
11: Hăpeṣ ʿeḇrôt ʾappekā: 'Let out the fury of your wrath' — ʿeḇrāh (fury, overflow of wrath): the cascading divine anger. ûrĕʾēh kol-gēʾeh wĕhašpîlēhû: 'look at everyone proud and humble him' — the administration of retributive justice God is inviting Job to attempt. If Job can do it, he wins. The point: no one can.
12: Rĕʾēh kol-gēʾeh haknaʿĕhû: 'Look at every proud person and bring him low' — the comprehensive justice Job has demanded. ûhădōk rĕšāʿîm taḥtām: 'tread down the wicked where they stand' — the punishment of wickedness in-place, immediate, comprehensive. This is what human justice cannot do.
13: Ṭomĕnēm bĕʿāpār yaḥad: 'Thrust them into the dust together' — the mass entombment of the wicked. Pĕnêhem ḥăboš bammistār: 'bind their faces in the realm of concealment' — mistār (hidden place, concealment): the hidden underworld. The challenge: can Job actually accomplish the cosmic moral administration he has demanded of God?
14: Wĕgam-ʾănî ʾôḏeh lāk: 'Then I myself will acknowledge you' — yāḏāh (to praise, acknowledge): God's own confession of praise if Job can do it. Kî-tôšîaʿ yĕmînĕkā lāk: 'that your own right hand can save you' — the condition of self-sufficiency: if Job has such power, he needs no one. The point is that he does not and cannot. The right hand that saves is YHWH's (Ps 98:1; Isa 63:5).
15: Rĕʾēh-nāʾ bĕhēmôt ʾăšer-ʿāśîtî ʿimmāk: 'Look at Behemoth, which I made just as I made you' — bĕhēmôt: plural of intensity from bĕhēmāh (beast, animal) — the super-beast. The identification is debated (hippopotamus most likely; others suggest a mythological creature). ʿĀśîtî ʿimmāk: 'I made alongside you' — Job and Behemoth are fellow-creatures of the same Creator.
16: Hinnēh nāʾ kōḥô bĕmotnāyw: 'Look at the strength in his haunches' — the anatomical survey of Behemoth's power. Wĕʾônô bišrîrê biṭnô: 'the power in the muscles of his belly' — ʾôn (strength, virility): the term for reproductive and physical potency. Behemoth's power is fundamentally bodily, concentrated in its core.
17: Yāpōẑ zĕnāḇô kĕʾārez: 'He swings his tail like a cedar' — the tail-comparison to a cedar tree suggests the large, powerful tail of a hippopotamus (not the stubby tail of an elephant, making 'elephant' identification less likely). Gîdê pāḥadāyw yĕśôrāgû: 'the sinews of his thighs are tightly woven' — the anatomical detail of the powerful thigh-muscles.
18: ʿAṣāmāyw ʾăpîqê nĕḥūšāh: 'His bones are like bronze pipes' — the mineral comparison for bone-density. Gĕrāmāyw kĕmôṭ barzel: 'his limbs are like bars of iron' — the dual mineral comparison (bronze/iron) establishes Behemoth's structural invulnerability to normal force.
19: Hûʾ rĕʾîšît darkê-ʾēl: 'He ranks first among God's works' — rĕʾîšît (first, chief): Behemoth as the premier example of divine creative power in the animal realm. Hāʿōśô yaggaš ḥarbô: 'only his Maker can approach him with a sword' — the exclusive divine prerogative over Behemoth. No human can subdue him; only God can.
20: Kî-yĕḇûl hārîm yiśśĕʾû-lô: 'The mountains produce food for him' — the scale of Behemoth's provisioning: mountains supply his diet. Kol-ḥayyat haśśāḏeh yĕśaḥăqû-šām: 'all the wild beasts play around him' — the paradox: the most powerful creature is surrounded by smaller animals that play near him without fear.
21: Taḥat ṣeʾĕlîm yiškāḇ: 'He rests beneath the lotus plants' — ṣeʾĕlîm (lotus plants, or perhaps a tree): the shade-vegetation of the Nile delta or Jordan valley, consistent with hippopotamus habitat. Bĕsēter qāneh ûḇiṣṣāh: 'sheltered in the reeds and swamps' — the marshy habitat of the hippo.
22: Yĕsukuhû ṣillālîm ṣalmāwet: 'The trees shade him with deep shadow' — ṣalmāwet (deep shadow, shadow of death): the word usually translated 'valley of the shadow of death' (Ps 23:4) here describes Behemoth's shade-canopy. ʿArĕḇê-naḥal yisabbĕḇûhû: 'willows of the brook encircle him' — the botanical precision of the riparian habitat.
23: Hēn yaʿăšōq nāhār lōʾ yaḥpôz: 'Even if the river floods, Behemoth is untroubled' — ʿāšaq (to oppress, overwhelm): flood-force does not move him. Yiḇṭaḥ kî-yāgîaḥ yardēn: 'he is at ease though the Jordan rushes into his mouth' — the Jordan at flood-force is merely a drink for Behemoth. The cosmic confidence of a creature designed for power in water.
24: Bĕʿênāyw yiqqāḥennû: 'Can anyone catch him while he watches?' — the rhetorical answer is no. Bĕmôqĕšîm yinnaq-ʾāp: 'can anyone trap him by piercing his nose?' — the standard animal-capture technique (nose-ring/rope): impossible with Behemoth. He cannot be mastered.
